focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 13

The impound office sat behind two layers of chain-link and a strip of razor wire that glinted like fishing line in the late-afternoon sun. The trailer was beige, dented around the doorframe, and cold from a wall unit that rattled as if somebody had dropped loose bolts in it. Eli stood at the counter with his green folder open to the insurance estimate and his thumb pressed over the number he had kept reading all week: **$5,110 less deductible**.

Renee, the clerk behind the scratched plexiglass, wore a denim vest over a thermal shirt and kept a peppermint tucked in her cheek. She had silver roots showing under drugstore red, and every time the printer jammed she tapped the side panel twice with the back of her knuckles before opening it. The move looked practiced, the way somebody knocks on old pipes before turning a faucet.

After finishing the dawn linen run in Glenwood, Eli had spent six hours on a shuttle to this Utah yard Mountain West used for western salvage, just to sign forms and collect what he thought was his only usable money.

"You're Eli Mercer?" she asked.

"Yeah. I talked to Mountain West this morning. They said the check was ready once I signed the release."

She scanned his license, frowned at her screen, and pushed the card back through the slot. "The check went to the lienholder three business days ago."

Eli blinked and stayed still long enough to feel the ache in his left rib sharpen against the edge of the counter. "No. The car was totaled. This was settlement money."

"Right," Renee said. "Settlement money. There is an active title claim. RidgeLine Title Finance. They filed first-position lien rights on the VIN. We paid lienholder direct."

He laughed once, thin and sharp. "Paid them what? All of it?"

Renee slid two stapled packets...
prev_tail_excerpt:
...temporarily functioning. He did not look redeemed. The distinction mattered.

When the evening check-ins started, he went back to the desk and greeted guests with the practiced politeness he had used in hospitals, in meetings, in any room where panic needed to be translated into procedure. He took IDs, handed keys, described the breakfast hours. His voice stayed level.

Between guests he touched the rubber band on his wrist, felt the note beneath it, and remembered the way Marisol had said say it back.

He had said it back. He would have to keep saying it back in actions.

Near midnight, the lot emptied again and the mountains disappeared into black shape against black sky. Eli logged the final check-in, set the pen down, and sat for a moment with his hands around a paper cup of bad coffee.

The flip phone lay beside the register. A tiny icon blinked: one new voicemail.

He pressed play.

Nora's voice came thin through the cheap speaker, all business and no buffer. "Eli, it's Nora. I need six-forty for June's meal plan by Friday at three p.m. Eastern or they cut her to ten swipes for the week. Mortgage cure deadline is October fifteenth, and they're asking nineteen hundred to hold the file. Text me by noon tomorrow with the exact transfer amount. Exact."

The line clicked dead. Eli replayed it once, then opened his pay envelope on the desk and counted out four hundred dollars in twenties with his thumb shaking against the bills.

He dialed Nora. Voicemail.

"It's Eli," he said. "I heard you. I'm wiring four hundred at opening tomorrow and I'll send the rest for June next Friday. I'll text the receipt by ten your time."

He ended the call, wrote `Fri 3 p.m. ET - June 640` across the back of Marisol's sticky note, and slid the note back under the rubber band on his wrist.
